titleStmt.title: Letter from Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery to Walter Long, 31 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a letter from Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery (1844-1924) to Walter Long (1854-1924). Montgomery was a lawyer and politician. Originally a Liberal and a strong supporter of Gladstone, Montgomery was also a firm Unionist, but by 1916 he believed that Ulster Unionists had no choice but to accept Lloyd George's proposal for a six-county Northern Ireland. Walter Long, 1st Viscount was born in Bath, Somerset, England on 13 July 1854. He was elected as a conservative MP in 1880 and held various positions within the government from the next 20+ years. In 1905 he was appointed as chief secretary of Ireland and in 1916 was president of the local government board. The letter refers to a piece written by Montgomery on the current political situation. It was been intended for the 'The Times' but Montgomery has decided just to send copies to prominent Unionists like Long instead.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Copy 31st May,1916 My Dear Long, I dictated enclosed letter to let off steam. Some
mutual friends of ours urged me to send it to the "Times".
I have, however, come to the conclusion that it is better not
to do so, but to send copies of it to a few friends to use if
they think it worth while, to show what the views of sound
Unionists over here are. Yours very truly,
